chunk_summary: Follow-up on a gluten-free diet emphasizes growth monitoring, serology trends, micronutrient evaluation, and troubleshooting persistent symptoms with a structured timeline.
learning_objectives: Understand the structured follow-up schedule for patients on a gluten-free diet.
Identify key micronutrients and laboratory tests for monitoring celiac disease management.
Learn troubleshooting strategies for persistent symptoms despite a gluten-free diet.
key_facts: Follow-up visits are scheduled every 3–6 months initially, then every 6 months until IgA tissue transglutaminase antibodies normalize, and every 12–24 months thereafter.
Baseline evaluation includes complete blood count, iron status, vitamin B12, vitamin D, and liver enzymes (e.g., ALT).
Moderate evidence suggests reduced seroconversion to hepatitis B vaccination in some celiac patients; antibody levels should be measured and boosters given if inadequate.
Persistent symptoms should first be assessed for cross-contact or hidden gluten exposure before considering alternative causes like lactose intolerance or functional pain disorders.
In India, ultra-processed gluten-free substitutes may worsen dietary quality; counseling should focus on naturally gluten-free staples and fiber adequacy.
reference_quote: Follow-up on a gluten-free diet should be growth-first and serology-linked, with structured visits every 3–6 months initially and subsequent intervals based on antibody normalization. (ESPGHAN, 2022)
